era_context: Late 15th century: Portuguese maritime expansion accelerating; Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean; Italian Renaissance flourishing; Ming China under the Hongwu and Yongle successors; Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl; the Iberian Reconquista in its final phase; England under Henry VII consolidating Tudor rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Interior Southeast Asia (limited European contact; local chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Henry VII defeats Lambert Simnel at the Battle of Stoke Field
events.description: King Henry VII of England defeated the forces of Lambert Simnel, a pretender to the English throne, at the Battle of Stoke Field on June 16, 1487. Simnel, a young man of humble origins who had been coached to impersonate Edward, Earl of Warwick (a potential rival claimant), had invaded England with support from Margaret of York (sister of Edward IV) and Irish and Scottish allies. The battle was the last major engagement of the Wars of the Roses and solidified Henry VII's hold on the throne. Simnel was captured and later pardoned, becoming a falconer in the royal household.

sources.name: Contemporary English chronicles (e.g., Polydore Vergil, Historia Anglica)

events.certainty_note: The battle and its outcome are well-documented in English historical records.

events.title: Diogo Cão's expedition reaches the mouth of the Congo River
events.description: Portuguese explorer Diogo Cão (also known as Diego Cao) led an expedition that reached the mouth of the Congo River in West-Central Africa. Cão's voyages, which began in 1482, were part of Portugal's systematic exploration of the African coast under the patronage of King John II. Cão erected stone pillars (padrões) to mark Portuguese claims and establish diplomatic contacts with local rulers. These expeditions expanded European knowledge of African geography and established the foundation for later Portuguese colonial activities.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and navigational accounts
Later Portuguese chronicles and historiographic accounts

events.certainty_note: Cão's expeditions are well-documented; the specific dating of 1487 for reaching the Congo is based on historiographic consensus, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet continues operations in the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He continued his series of massive maritime expeditions (the Treasure Voyages) under the Ming Dynasty, exploring and establishing tributary relationships across the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and Southeast Asia. By 1487, Zheng He's expeditions had already fundamentally mapped the Indian Ocean and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks across Asia and Africa. These voyages represented the most advanced naval technology and organization of the era.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Dynasty patron, deceased 1424; continued under successors)

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The broader Treasure Voyage program is well-documented; specific activities in 1487 are less precisely dated but consistent with the documented expedition schedule.

events.title: Ahuitzotl's military campaigns continue; Aztec Empire expansion
events.description: Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) continued his aggressive military campaigns, expanding the Aztec Empire through conquest and tributary relationships. By 1487, the empire controlled much of central Mexico and was undertaking major construction projects in Tenochtitlan. Ahuitzotl was known for his military prowess and for the expansion of the Templo Mayor, which was completed and dedicated in 1487 with elaborate ceremonies and mass sacrifices.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (compiled post-conquest but based on pre-Columbian sources)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (16th-century compilation of Nahua accounts)

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's reign and general military activities are well-attested; specific events in 1487 are less precisely dated due to reliance on post-conquest sources. The dedication of the Templo Mayor is traditionally dated to 1487, though some sources place it in 1486 or 1488.

events.title: Ottoman-Mamluk tensions escalate in the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Tensions between the Ottoman Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt escalated in 1487, as both powers competed for control of trade routes and territorial influence in the eastern Mediterranean and the Levant. These tensions would eventually lead to the Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491), which resulted in Ottoman victory and the incorporation of the Levant and Egypt into the Ottoman Empire. The conflict marked a significant shift in the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Ottoman and Mamluk chronicles and diplomatic records
Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Ottoman and Mamluk empires)

events.certainty_note: The broader Ottoman-Mamluk conflict is well-documented; specific events in 1487 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Stoke Field is sometimes portrayed as the definitive end of the Wars of the Roses; modern scholarship emphasizes that Henry VII's position remained somewhat precarious for several more years, and that the battle was significant but not conclusive. The dating of Diogo Cão's expedition to the Congo is uncertain; some sources place it in 1485 or 1486. The dedication of the Templo Mayor is traditionally dated to 1487, but some sources place it in 1486 or 1488, reflecting the uncertainty of Aztec chronology based on post-conquest sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1487 is rich for Europe (particularly England, Portugal, and the Ottoman Empire) due to extensive diplomatic archives, royal records, and contemporary chronicles. Asian sources (Ming China, Ottoman Empire) are also well-preserved. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is less well-attested. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific remain poorly documented for this year. Mesoamerican history relies heavily on post-conquest compilations and codices, which introduce chronological uncertainty.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Stoke Field was part of Henry VII's consolidation of power after his accession in 1485
Diogo Cão's expeditions were contemporary with Bartolomeu Dias's voyage, both part of Portuguese exploration
Escalating tensions in 1487 led to the Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491)

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
